content:
16 min read Just now -- Press enter or click to view image in full size Understanding Props and PropTypes in React: Let’s first understand the difference between export default vs named export in React. What are exports? In React, we use exports to share code from one file to another. There are two main types: 1. export default 2. export (named export) What is ‘export default’? What it means: A file can have only ONE default export It is used when: · You want to export main thing from a file Example: a basic example. Create a new file named, Module1.mjs: Press enter or click to view image in full size And one another file named, Modul2.mjs: Press enter or click to view image in full size Now, in terminal try to run the Module2.mjs using the node: What this output indicates about the ‘export default’? It exports one main value from a file. Even though you had 3 variables (a, b, c), only this one was exported: export default a; (look at line 5 of Module1.mjs). Also, we can name the import anything you want, here we wrote ‘import any_name’ but still got the ‘Aditya’. So this shows: default export is NOT bound to a name. Only ONE default export is allowed per file, if one write like this in Module1.mjs: Press enter or click to view image in full size And then try to run the node: it will give the error. Press enter or click to view image in full size Now, let’s talk about named exports. What are ‘named export’? What it means: You can export multiple things from a file. Example: Module1.mjs: Press enter or click to view image in full size While Moduel2.mjs: Press enter or click to view image in full size Now, in the terminal: Press enter or click to view image in full size What you learn from this output about ‘named export’? · Names are FIXED in named exports, we must use the exact same names. · You can import only what you need · Multiple exports are allowed (unlike default) · You can rename while importing (using ‘as’) Press enter or click to view image in full size How does they differ from each other? Can we use both together? Yes, we can use both ‘export default’ and ‘named export’ together. Example: Module1.mjs: Module2.mjs: Press enter or click to view image in full size Terminal: Press enter or click to view image in full size Now, let’s talk about the “Props”….! What are Props? Props = Properties. In simple words: Props are used to pass data from one component to another in React. Think like: · Function arguments · Input to a component Why Props are needed? Without props: · Every component shows same data · No flexibility With props: · We can send different data to same component Example: a basic example without props (using no extra file) App.js: Press enter or click to view image in full size Terminal: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size Now, the same example using the props: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size Can we use props to pass other datatypes except string? Yes, we can do so. · String → “text” · Number → {20} Example: App.js: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size What if the default behaviour of props? App.js: (here we just emptied the passed value, it is not default behaviour example) Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size Example: default behaviour. Props have no built-in default values. If missing → undefined. App.js: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size How to handle the missing prop of above example? We can use default props to do so. Example: App.js: (modern way) Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size Example: Default inside function body App.js: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size Example: defaultProps (older approach) App.js: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Discouraged Now, let’s understand about passing JSX with props: How to pass HTML code via JSX? children is the recommended React pattern for passing markup from a parent component to a child component. Example: Sample.jsx: Press enter or click to view image in full size App.jsx: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size Now, suppose we want to make the colour of the text dynamic as well: App.jsx: Press enter or click to view image in full size In this code, the App component renders the Sample component twice. The first Sample receives a color prop with the value “Pink” and displays the heading “Hello, Aditya” as its children, so the text appears in pink. The second Sample is rendered without a color prop and displays “Hello, Utsav” as its children. Since both headings are passed between the opening and closing tags, they are received through the children prop and rendered inside the
defined in the Sample component. This demonstrates how React components can be reused with different content and props to produce different outputs. Sample..jsx: Press enter or click to view image in full size Sample is a reusable React functional component that accepts two props: children and color. The children prop represents any JSX content placed between the opening and closing tags when the component is used, and it is rendered inside the
. The color prop is used to dynamically set the text color through inline styling. The component also applies a fixed green border and a width of 300px, making it a simple wrapper that can display different content and text colors based on the props passed to it. Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size Now, suppose we want to make Get Input Field Value, I meant suppose we want to see the text user entered dynamically below the text box. App.jsx: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: when we begin typing in the box Press enter or click to view image in full size This code demonstrates how to use React’s useState hook to capture and display the value entered into an input field. The state variable val is initialized with “Aditya”. Whenever the user types in the input box, the onChange event is triggered, and setVal(event.target.value) updates the state with the current input value. Since React re-renders the component whenever state changes, the

{val}

element immediately displays the latest text entered by the user. This creates a real-time connection between the input field and the displayed value, a pattern known as a controlled component in React. Now, suppose we want that the value in both the input field and the text displayed below it get deleted when we click on “clear” button, then we can use this: App.jsx: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: Press enter or click to view image in full size Output: when clicked on the button Press enter or click to view image in full size Understanding Controlled Componenta: What are Controlled Components? A controlled component in React is a form element (such as an ,
